sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 126, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Agent at rows 12-17 (top), blocks at rows 25-28 (red), 31-34 (blue), 37-40 (green). Blocks move DOWN when agent moves DOWN, but not UP when agent moves UP. Agent's range seems limited.
- Plan: Try moving agent DOWN to see if range expanded after blocks moved. Also try ACTION7 to see if it enables new movement.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 125 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key findings:
- DOWNx6: gameplay_changed=False (only 3 DOWN executed)
- Agent at rows 18-23, blocks at rows 25-28 (red), 31-34 (blue), 37-40 (green)
- Agent can't move further DOWN from rows 18-23
- Blocks didn't move (same positions as before)
So the agent's range is rows 12-23. The agent can move between rows 12-17 and rows 18-23.
The blocks are at rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40, which are below the agent's range.
The agent can never reach the blocks because they're below the agent's range.
Let me reconsider the game. Maybe the goal is not to move the agent to the blocks.
Looking at the palette at the bottom: agent - red - green - blue
Looking at the panel: red - blue - green
The palette order is red, green, blue. The panel order is red, blue, green.
Maybe the goal is to match the panel order to the palette order.
But how? The blocks move DOWN when the agent moves DOWN, but they maintain their relative order.
Unless... the blocks can be rearranged by some other mechanism.
Let me think about ACTION7. Earlier, ACTION7 retracted the trail. But maybe it does something else too.
Let me try ACTION7 and see what happens to the blocks.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game mechanics.
The agent is at the left side of the panel. The trail extends to the right.
The blocks are at the right side of the panel.
The palette shows the blocks in a specific order.
Maybe the goal is to have the trail touch the blocks in the correct order.
The trail is horizontal, at the agent's row. When the agent is at rows 12-17, the trail is at rows 12-13.
The red block is at rows 25-28. The trail is above the red block.
When the agent moves DOWN to rows 18-23, the trail is at rows 18-19. The red block is at rows 25-28. The trail is still above the red block.
So the trail never touches the blocks because the agent's range is rows 12-23, and the blocks are at rows 25-40.
Wait, but earlier when the agent was at the initial position (rows 36-41), the blocks were at rows 19-22, 25-28, 31-34.
The agent was at rows 36-41, and the green block was at rows 31-34. The agent was below the green block.
The trail was at rows 36-37 (top of agent). The green block was at rows 31-34. The trail was below the green block.
So the trail never touches the blocks in any position.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to have the agent at a specific position relative to the blocks.
Or maybe the goal is to have the trail at a specific position.
Actually, let me reconsider the ACTION7 mechanic. ACTION7 retracts the trail. Maybe the trail's length is important.
When the trail is extended (RIGHT), it goes from the agent to the right edge of the panel.
When the trail is retracted (ACTION7), it disappears.
Maybe the goal is to have the trail touch the blocks.
But the trail is horizontal, and the blocks are to the right of the agent. The trail extends from the agent to the right edge of the panel.
The blocks are at cols 42-45, and the trail is at cols 17-46. So the trail overlaps the blocks horizontally.
But the trail is at the agent's row, and the blocks are at different rows. So the trail doesn't overlap the blocks vertically.
Unless... the trail can be moved vertically by moving the agent UP/DOWN.
When the agent is at rows 12-17, the trail is at rows 12-13.
When the agent is at rows 18-23, the trail is at rows 18-19.
The red block is at rows 25-28. The trail is at rows 18-19 when the agent is at rows 18-23.
The trail is 7 rows above the red block.
So the trail never touches the blocks.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is not about the trail touching the blocks.
Maybe the goal is about the agent's position relative to the blocks.
Or maybe the goal is about the order of the blocks.
Let me try a different approach. Let me try using MOUSE to click on the blocks or the palette.
The palette shows: agent - red - green - blue
The panel shows: red - blue - green
Maybe I need to click on the blocks to rearrange them.
Let me try clicking on the red block first.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Agent range is rows 12-23, blocks at rows 25-40. Agent can't reach blocks. Trail overlaps blocks horizontally but not vertically.
Plan: Try MOUSE to click on blocks or palette to see if clicking rearranges blocks.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the red block (rows 25-28, cols 42-45)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 26, 'col': 43}])
print(f"Click red block: gameplay_changed={result.get('gameplay_changed')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

# Check block positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('R', 'b', 'N') and n['pixels'] == 16:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min, r_max = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c_min, c_max = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"  {n['color']} rows={r_min}-{r_max} cols={c_min}-{c_max}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Click red block: gameplay_changed=False, level_completed=False
  R rows=25-28 cols=42-45
  b rows=31-34 cols=42-45
  N rows=37-40 cols=42-45
  R rows=57-60 cols=27-30
  N rows=57-60 cols=33-36
  b rows=57-60 cols=39-42
